html:
VIII

A Few Grains of Pepper

The dollsвАЩ dressmaker went no more to the business-premises of Pubsey and Co. in St.¬†Mary Axe, after chance had disclosed to her (as she supposed) the flinty and hypocritical character of Mr.¬†Riah. She often moralized over her work on the tricks and the manners of that venerable cheat, but made her little purchases elsewhere, and lived a secluded life. After much consultation with herself, she decided not to put Lizzie Hexam on her guard against the old man, arguing that the disappointment of finding him out would come upon her quite soon enough. Therefore, in her communication with her friend by letter, she was silent on this theme, and principally dilated on the backslidings of her bad child, who every day grew worse and worse.

вАЬYou wicked old boy,вАЭ Miss Wren would say to him, with a menacing forefinger, вАЬyouвАЩll force me to run away from you, after all, you will; and then youвАЩll shake to bits, and thereвАЩll be nobody to pick up the pieces!вАЭ

At this foreshadowing of a desolate decease, the wicked old boy would whine and whimper, and would sit shaking himself into the lowest of low spirits, until such time as he could shake himself out of the house and shake another threepennyworth into himself. But dead drunk or dead sober (he had come to such a pass that he was least alive in the latter state), it was always on the conscience of the paralytic scarecrow that he had betrayed his sharp parent for sixty threepennyworths of rum, which were all gone, and that her sharpness would infallibly detect his having done it, sooner or later. All things considered therefore, and addition made of the state of his body to the state of his mind, the bed on which Mr.¬†Dolls reposed was a bed of roses from which the flowers and leaves had entirely faded, leaving him to lie upon the thorns and stalks.

On a certain day, Miss Wren was alone at her work, with the house-door set open for coolness, and was trolling in a small sweet voice a mournful little song which might have been the song of the doll she was dressing, bemoaning the brittleness and meltability of wax, when whom should she descry standing on the pavement, looking in at her, but Mr.¬†Fledgeby.

вАЬI thought it was you?вАЭ said Fledgeby, coming up the two steps.

вАЬDid you?вАЭ Miss Wren retorted. вАЬAnd I thought it was you, young man. Quite a coincidence. YouвАЩre not mistaken, and IвАЩm not mistaken. How clever we are!вАЭ

вАЬWell, and how are you?вАЭ said Fledgeby.

вАЬI am pretty much as usual, sir,вАЭ replied Miss Wren. вАЬA very unfortunate parent, worried out of my life and senses by a very bad child.вАЭ

FledgebyвАЩs small eyes opened so wide that they might have passed for ordinary-sized eyes, as he stared about him for the very young person whom he supposed to be in question.

вАЬBut youвАЩre not a parent,вАЭ said Miss Wren, вАЬand consequently itвАЩs of no use talking to you upon a family subject.вБ†вАФTo what am I to attribute the honour and favour?вАЭ

вАЬTo a wish to improve your acquaintance,вАЭ Mr.¬†Fledgeby replied.

Miss Wren, stopping to bite her thread, looked at him very knowingly.

вАЬWe never meet now,вАЭ said Fledgeby; вАЬdo we?вАЭ

вАЬNo,вАЭ said Miss Wren, chopping off the word.

вАЬSo I had a mind,вАЭ pursued Fledgeby, вАЬto come and have a talk with you about our dodging friend, the child of Israel.вАЭ

вАЬSo he gave you my address; did he?вАЭ asked Miss Wren.

вАЬI got it out of him,вАЭ said Fledgeby, with a stammer.

вАЬYou seem to see a good deal of him,вАЭ remarked Miss Wren, with shrewd distrust. вАЬA good deal of him you seem to see, considering.вАЭ

вАЬYes, I do,вАЭ said Fledgeby. вАЬConsidering.вАЭ

вАЬHavenвАЩt you,вАЭ inquired the dressmaker, bending over the doll on which her art was being exercised, вАЬdone interceding with him yet?вАЭ

вАЬNo,вАЭ said Fledgeby, shaking his head.

вАЬLa! Been interceding with him all this time, and sticking to him still?вАЭ said Miss Wren, busy with her work.

вАЬSticking to him is the word,вАЭ said Fledgeby.

Miss Wren pursued her occupation with a concentrated air, and asked, after an interval of silent industry:

вАЬAre you in the army?вАЭ

вАЬNot exactly,вАЭ said Fledgeby, rather flattered by the question.

вАЬNavy?вАЭ asked Miss Wren.

вАЬNвБ†вАФno,вАЭ said Fledgeby. He qualified these two negatives, as if he were not absolutely in either service, but was almost in both.

вАЬWhat are you then?вАЭ demanded Miss Wren.

вАЬI am a gentleman, I am,вАЭ said Fledgeby.

вАЬOh!вАЭ assented Jenny, screwing up her mouth with an appearance of conviction. вАЬYes, to be sure! That accounts for your having so much time to give to interceding. But only to think how kind and friendly a gentleman you must be!вАЭ

Mr.¬†Fledgeby found that he was skating round a board marked Dangerous, and had better cut out a fresh track. вАЬLetвАЩs get back to the dodgerest of the dodgers,вАЭ said he. вАЬWhatвАЩs he up to in the case of your friend the handsome gal? He must have some object. WhatвАЩs his object?вАЭ

вАЬCannot undertake to say, sir, I am sure!вАЭ returned Miss Wren, composedly.

вАЬHe wonвАЩt acknowledge where sheвАЩs gone,вАЭ said Fledgeby; вАЬand I have a fancy that I should like to have another look at her. Now I know he knows where she is gone.вАЭ

вАЬCannot undertake to say, sir, I am sure!вАЭ Miss Wren again rejoined.

вАЬAnd you know where she is gone,вАЭ hazarded Fledgeby.

вАЬCannot undertake to say, sir, really,вАЭ replied Miss Wren.

The quaint little chin met Mr.¬†FledgebyвАЩs gaze with such a baffling hitch, that that agreeable gentleman was for some time at a loss how to resume his fascinating part in the dialogue. At length he said:

вАЬMiss Jenny!вБ†вАФThatвАЩs your name, if I donвАЩt mistake?вАЭ

вАЬProbably you donвАЩt mistake, sir,вАЭ was Miss WrenвАЩs cool answer; вАЬbecause you had it on the best authority. Mine, you know.вАЭ

вАЬMiss Jenny! Instead of coming up and being dead, letвАЩs come out and look alive. ItвАЩll pay better, I assure you,вАЭ said Fledgeby, bestowing an inveigling twinkle or two upon the dressmaker. вАЬYouвАЩll find it pay better.вАЭ

вАЬPerhaps,вАЭ said Miss Jenny, holding out her doll at armвАЩs length, and critically contemplating the effect of her art with her scissors on her lips and her head thrown back, as if her interest lay there, and not in the conversation; вАЬperhaps youвАЩll explain your meaning, young man, which is Greek to me.вБ†вАФYou must have another touch of blue in your trimming, my dear.вАЭ Having addressed the last remark to her fair client, Miss Wren proceeded to snip at some blue fragments that lay before her, among fragments of all colours, and to thread a needle from a skein of blue silk.

вАЬLook here,вАЭ said Fledgeby.вБ†вАФвАЬAre you attending?вАЭ

вАЬI am attending, sir,вАЭ replied Miss Wren, without the slightest appearance of so doing. вАЬAnother touch of blue in your trimming, my dear.вАЭ

вАЬWell, look here,вАЭ said Fledgeby, rather discouraged by the circumstances under which he found himself pursuing the conversation. вАЬIf youвАЩre attendingвБ†вАФвАЭ

(вАЬLight blue, my sweet young lady,вАЭ remarked Miss Wren, in a sprightly tone, вАЬbeing best suited to your fair complexion and your flaxen curls.вАЭ)

вАЬI say, if youвАЩre attending,вАЭ proceeded Fledgeby, вАЬitвАЩll pay better in this way. ItвАЩll lead in a roundabout manner to your buying damage and waste of Pubsey and Co. at a nominal price, or even getting it for nothing.вАЭ

вАЬAha!вАЭ thought the dressmaker. вАЬBut you are not so roundabout, Little Eyes, that I donвАЩt notice your answering for Pubsey and Co. after all! Little Eyes, Little Eyes, youвАЩre too cunning by half.вАЭ

вАЬAnd I take it for granted,вАЭ pursued Fledgeby, вАЬthat to get the most of your materials for nothing would be well worth your while, Miss Jenny?вАЭ

вАЬYou may take it for granted,вАЭ returned the dressmaker with many knowing nods, вАЬthat itвАЩs always well worth my while to make money.вАЭ

вАЬNow,вАЭ said Fledgeby approvingly, вАЬyouвАЩre answering to a sensible purpose. Now, youвАЩre coming out and looking alive! So I make so free, Miss Jenny, as to offer the remark, that you and Judah were too thick together to last. You canвАЩt come to be intimate with such a deep file as Judah without beginning to see a little way into him, you know,вАЭ said Fledgeby with a wink.

вАЬI must own,вАЭ returned the dressmaker, with her eyes upon her work, вАЬthat we are not good friends at present.вАЭ

вАЬI know youвАЩre not good friends at present,вАЭ said Fledgeby. вАЬI know all about it. I should like to pay off Judah, by not letting him have his own deep way in everything. In most things heвАЩll get it by hook or by crook, butвБ†вАФhang it all!вБ†вАФdonвАЩt let him have his own deep way in everything. ThatвАЩs too much.вАЭ Mr.¬†Fledgeby said this with some display of indignant warmth, as if he was counsel in the cause for Virtue.

вАЬHow can I prevent his having his own way?вАЭ began the dressmaker.

вАЬDeep way, I called it,вАЭ said Fledgeby.

вАЬвБ†вАФHis own deep way, in anything?вАЭ

вАЬIвАЩll tell you,вАЭ said Fledgeby. вАЬI like to hear you ask it, because itвАЩs looking alive. ItвАЩs what I should expect to find in one of your sagacious understanding. Now, candidly.вАЭ

вАЬEh?вАЭ cried Miss Jenny.

вАЬI said, now candidly,вАЭ Mr.¬†Fledgeby explained, a little put out.

вАЬOh-h!вАЭ

вАЬI should be glad to countermine him, respecting the handsome gal, your friend. He means something there. You may depend upon it, Judah means something there. He has a motive, and of course his motive is a dark motive. Now, whatever his motive is, itвАЩs necessary to his motiveвАЭвБ†вАФMr.¬†FledgebyвАЩs constructive powers were not equal to the avoidance of some tautology hereвБ†вАФвАЬthat it should be kept from me, what he has done with her. So I put it to you, who know: What has he done with her? I ask no more. And is that asking much, when you understand that it will pay?вАЭ

Miss Jenny Wren, who had cast her eyes upon the bench again after her last interruption, sat looking at it, needle in hand but not working, for some moments. She then briskly resumed her work, and said with a sidelong glance of her eyes and chin at Mr.¬†Fledgeby:

вАЬWhere dвАЩye live?вАЭ

вАЬAlbany, Piccadilly,вАЭ replied Fledgeby.

вАЬWhen are you at home?вАЭ

вАЬWhen you like.вАЭ

вАЬBreakfast-time?вАЭ said Jenny, in her abruptest and shortest manner.

вАЬNo better time in the day,вАЭ said Fledgeby.

вАЬIвАЩll look in upon you tomorrow, young man. Those two ladies,вАЭ pointing to dolls, вАЬhave an appointment in Bond Street at ten precisely. When IвАЩve dropped вАЩem there, IвАЩll drive round to you.вАЭ With a weird little laugh, Miss Jenny pointed to her crutch-stick as her equipage.

вАЬThis is looking alive indeed!вАЭ cried Fledgeby, rising.

вАЬMark you! I promise you nothing,вАЭ said the dollsвАЩ dressmaker, dabbing two dabs at him with her needle, as if she put out both his eyes.

вАЬNo no. I understand,вАЭ returned Fledgeby. вАЬThe damage and waste question shall be settled first. It shall be made to pay; donвАЩt you be afraid. Good day, Miss Jenny.вАЭ

вАЬGood day, young man.вАЭ

Mr.¬†FledgebyвАЩs prepossessing form withdrew itself; and the little dressmaker, clipping and snipping and stitching, and stitching and snipping and clipping, fell to work at a great rate; musing and muttering all the time.

вАЬMisty, misty, misty. CanвАЩt make it out. Little Eyes and the wolf in a conspiracy? Or Little Eyes and the wolf against one another? CanвАЩt make it out. My poor Lizzie, have they both designs against you, either way? CanвАЩt make it out. Is Little Eyes Pubsey, and the wolf Co? CanвАЩt make it out. Pubsey true to Co, and Co to Pubsey? Pubsey false to Co, and Co to Pubsey? CanвАЩt make it out. What said Little Eyes? вАШNow, candidly?вАЩ Ah! However the cat jumps, heвАЩs a liar. ThatвАЩs all I can make out at present; but you may go to bed in the Albany, Piccadilly, with that for your pillow, young man!вАЭ Thereupon, the little dressmaker again dabbed out his eyes separately, and making a loop in the air of her thread and deftly catching it into a knot with her needle, seemed to bowstring him into the bargain.

For the terrors undergone by Mr.¬†Dolls that evening when his little parent sat profoundly meditating over her work, and when he imagined himself found out, as often as she changed her attitude, or turned her eyes towards him, there is no adequate name. Moreover it was her habit to shake her head at that wretched old boy whenever she caught his eye as he shivered and shook. What are popularly called вАЬthe tremblesвАЭ being in full force upon him that evening, and likewise what are popularly called вАЬthe horrors,вАЭ he had a very bad time of it; which was not made better by his being so remorseful as frequently to moan вАЬSixty threepennorths.вАЭ This imperfect sentence not being at all intelligible as a confession, but sounding like a Gargantuan order for a dram, brought him into new difficulties by occasioning his parent to pounce at him in a more than usually snappish manner, and to overwhelm him with bitter reproaches.

What was a bad time for Mr.¬†Dolls, could not fail to be a bad time for the dollsвАЩ dressmaker. However, she was on the alert next morning, and drove to Bond Street, and set down the two ladies punctually, and then directed her equipage to conduct her to the Albany. Arrived at the doorway of the house in which Mr.¬†FledgebyвАЩs chambers were, she found a lady standing there in a travelling dress, holding in her handвБ†вАФof all things in the worldвБ†вАФa gentlemanвАЩs hat.

вАЬYou want someone?вАЭ said the lady in a stern manner.

вАЬI am going upstairs to Mr.¬†FledgebyвАЩs.вАЭ

вАЬYou cannot do that at this moment. There is a gentleman with him. I am waiting for the gentleman. His business with Mr.¬†Fledgeby will very soon be transacted, and then you can go up. Until the gentleman comes down, you must wait here.вАЭ

While speaking, and afterwards, the lady kept watchfully between her and the staircase, as if prepared to oppose her going up, by force. The lady being of a stature to stop her with a hand, and looking mightily determined, the dressmaker stood still.

вАЬWell? Why do you listen?вАЭ asked the lady.

вАЬI am not listening,вАЭ said the dressmaker.

вАЬWhat do you hear?вАЭ asked the lady, altering her phrase.

вАЬIs it a kind of a spluttering somewhere?вАЭ said the dressmaker, with an inquiring look.

вАЬMr.¬†Fledgeby in his shower-bath, perhaps,вАЭ remarked the lady, smiling.

вАЬAnd somebodyвАЩs beating a carpet, I think?вАЭ

вАЬMr.¬†FledgebyвАЩs carpet, I dare say,вАЭ replied the smiling lady.

Miss Wren had a reasonably good eye for smiles, being well accustomed to them on the part of her young friends, though their smiles mostly ran smaller than in nature. But she had never seen so singular a smile as that upon this ladyвАЩs face. It twitched her nostrils open in a remarkable manner, and contracted her lips and eyebrows. It was a smile of enjoyment too, though of such a fierce kind that Miss Wren thought she would rather not enjoy herself than do it in that way.

вАЬWell!вАЭ said the lady, watching her. вАЬWhat now?вАЭ

вАЬI hope thereвАЩs nothing the matter!вАЭ said the dressmaker.

вАЬWhere?вАЭ inquired the lady.

вАЬI donвАЩt know where,вАЭ said Miss Wren, staring about her. вАЬBut I never heard such odd noises. DonвАЩt you think I had better call somebody?вАЭ

вАЬI think you had better not,вАЭ returned the lady with a significant frown, and drawing closer.

On this hint, the dressmaker relinquished the idea, and stood looking at the lady as hard as the lady looked at her. Meanwhile the dressmaker listened with amazement to the odd noises which still continued, and the lady listened too, but with a coolness in which there was no trace of amazement.

Soon afterwards, came a slamming and banging of doors; and then came running downstairs, a gentleman with whiskers, and out of breath, who seemed to be red-hot.

вАЬIs your business done, Alfred?вАЭ inquired the lady.

вАЬVery thoroughly done,вАЭ replied the gentleman, as he took his hat from her.

вАЬYou can go up to Mr.¬†Fledgeby as soon as you like,вАЭ said the lady, moving haughtily away.

вАЬOh! And you can take these three pieces of stick with you,вАЭ added the gentleman politely, вАЬand say, if you please, that they come from Mr.¬†Alfred Lammle, with his compliments on leaving England. Mr.¬†Alfred Lammle. Be so good as not to forget the name.вАЭ

The three pieces of stick were three broken and frayed fragments of a stout lithe cane. Miss Jenny taking them wonderingly, and the gentleman repeating with a grin, вАЬMr.¬†Alfred Lammle, if youвАЩll be so good. Compliments, on leaving England,вАЭ the lady and gentleman walked away quite deliberately, and Miss Jenny and her crutch-stick went upstairs. вАЬLammle, Lammle, Lammle?вАЭ Miss Jenny repeated as she panted from stair to stair, вАЬwhere have I heard that name? Lammle, Lammle? I know! Saint Mary Axe!вАЭ

With a gleam of new intelligence in her sharp face, the dollsвАЩ dressmaker pulled at FledgebyвАЩs bell. No one answered; but, from within the chambers, there proceeded a continuous spluttering sound of a highly singular and unintelligible nature.

вАЬGood gracious! Is Little Eyes choking?вАЭ cried Miss Jenny.

Pulling at the bell again and getting no reply, she pushed the outer door, and found it standing ajar. No one being visible on her opening it wider, and the spluttering continuing, she took the liberty of opening an inner door, and then beheld the extraordinary spectacle of Mr.¬†Fledgeby in a shirt, a pair of Turkish trousers, and a Turkish cap, rolling over and over on his own carpet, and spluttering wonderfully.

вАЬOh Lord!вАЭ gasped Mr.¬†Fledgeby. вАЬOh my eye! Stop thief! I am strangling. Fire! Oh my eye! A glass of water. Give me a glass of water. Shut the door. Murder! Oh Lord!вАЭ And then rolled and spluttered more than ever.

Hurrying into another room, Miss Jenny got a glass of water, and brought it for FledgebyвАЩs relief: who, gasping, spluttering, and rattling in his throat betweenwhiles, drank some water, and laid his head faintly on her arm.

вАЬOh my eye!вАЭ cried Fledgeby, struggling anew. вАЬItвАЩs salt and snuff. ItвАЩs up my nose, and down my throat, and in my windpipe. Ugh! Ow! Ow! Ow! AhвБ†вАФhвБ†вАФhвБ†вАФh!вАЭ And here, crowing fearfully, with his eyes starting out of his head, appeared to be contending with every mortal disease incidental to poultry.

вАЬAnd oh my eye, IвАЩm so sore!вАЭ cried Fledgeby, starting, over on his back, in a spasmodic way that caused the dressmaker to retreat to the wall. вАЬOh I smart so! Do put something to my back and arms, and legs and shoulders. Ugh! ItвАЩs down my throat again and canвАЩt come up. Ow! Ow! Ow! AhвБ†вАФhвБ†вАФhвБ†вАФh! Oh I smart so!вАЭ Here Mr.¬†Fledgeby bounded up, and bounded down, and went rolling over and over again.

The dollsвАЩ dressmaker looked on until he rolled himself into a corner with his Turkish slippers uppermost, and then, resolving in the first place to address her ministration to the salt and snuff, gave him more water and slapped his back. But, the latter application was by no means a success, causing Mr.¬†Fledgeby to scream, and to cry out, вАЬOh my eye! donвАЩt slap me! IвАЩm covered with weales and I smart so!вАЭ

However, he gradually ceased to choke and crow, saving at intervals, and Miss Jenny got him into an easy-chair: where, with his eyes red and watery, with his features swollen, and with some half-dozen livid bars across his face, he presented a most rueful sight.

вАЬWhat ever possessed you to take salt and snuff, young man?вАЭ inquired Miss Jenny.

вАЬI didnвАЩt take it,вАЭ the dismal youth replied. вАЬIt was crammed into my mouth.вАЭ

вАЬWho crammed it?вАЭ asked Miss Jenny.

вАЬHe did,вАЭ answered Fledgeby. вАЬThe assassin. Lammle. He rubbed it into my mouth and up my nose and down my throatвБ†вАФOw! Ow! Ow! AhвБ†вАФhвБ†вАФhвБ†вАФh! Ugh!вБ†вАФto prevent my crying out, and then cruelly assaulted me.вАЭ

вАЬWith this?вАЭ asked Miss Jenny, showing the pieces of cane.

вАЬThatвАЩs the weapon,вАЭ said Fledgeby, eyeing it with the air of an acquaintance. вАЬHe broke it over me. Oh I smart so! How did you come by it?вАЭ

вАЬWhen he ran downstairs and joined the lady he had left in the hall with his hatвАЭвБ†вАФMiss Jenny began.

вАЬOh!вАЭ groaned Mr.¬†Fledgeby, writhing, вАЬshe was holding his hat, was she? I might have known she was in it.вАЭ

вАЬWhen he came downstairs and joined the lady who wouldnвАЩt let me come up, he gave me the pieces for you, and I was to say, вАШWith Mr.¬†Alfred LammleвАЩs compliments on his leaving England.вАЩвАКвАЭ Miss Jenny said it with such spiteful satisfaction, and such a hitch of her chin and eyes as might have added to Mr.¬†FledgebyвАЩs miseries, if he could have noticed either, in his bodily pain with his hand to his head.

вАЬShall I go for the police?вАЭ inquired Miss Jenny, with a nimble start towards the door.

вАЬStop! No, donвАЩt!вАЭ cried Fledgeby. вАЬDonвАЩt, please. We had better keep it quiet. Will you be so good as shut the door? Oh I do smart so!вАЭ

In testimony of the extent to which he smarted, Mr.¬†Fledgeby came wallowing out of the easy-chair, and took another roll on the carpet.

вАЬNow the doorвАЩs shut,вАЭ said Mr.¬†Fledgeby, sitting up in anguish, with his Turkish cap half on and half off, and the bars on his face getting bluer, вАЬdo me the kindness to look at my back and shoulders. They must be in an awful state, for I hadnвАЩt got my dressing-gown on, when the brute came rushing in. Cut my shirt away from the collar; thereвАЩs a pair of scissors on that table. Oh!вАЭ groaned Mr.¬†Fledgeby, with his hand to his head again. вАЬHow I do smart, to be sure!вАЭ

вАЬThere?вАЭ inquired Miss Jenny, alluding to the back and shoulders.

вАЬOh Lord, yes!вАЭ moaned Fledgeby, rocking himself. вАЬAnd all over! Everywhere!вАЭ

The busy little dressmaker quickly snipped the shirt away, and laid bare the results of as furious and sound a thrashing as even Mr.¬†Fledgeby merited. вАЬYou may well smart, young man!вАЭ exclaimed Miss Jenny. And stealthily rubbed her little hands behind him, and poked a few exultant pokes with her two forefingers over the crown of his head.

вАЬWhat do you think of vinegar and brown paper?вАЭ inquired the suffering Fledgeby, still rocking and moaning. вАЬDoes it look as if vinegar and brown paper was the sort of application?вАЭ

вАЬYes,вАЭ said Miss Jenny, with a silent chuckle. вАЬIt looks as if it ought to be pickled.вАЭ

Mr.¬†Fledgeby collapsed under the word вАЬpickled,вАЭ and groaned again. вАЬMy kitchen is on this floor,вАЭ he said; вАЬyouвАЩll find brown paper in a dresser-drawer there, and a bottle of vinegar on a shelf. Would you have the kindness to make a few plasters and put вАЩem on? It canвАЩt be kept too quiet.вАЭ

вАЬOne, twoвБ†вАФhumвБ†вАФfive, six. YouвАЩll want six,вАЭ said the dressmaker.

вАЬThereвАЩs smart enough,вАЭ whimpered Mr.¬†Fledgeby, groaning and writhing again, вАЬfor sixty.вАЭ

Miss Jenny repaired to the kitchen, scissors in hand, found the brown paper and found the vinegar, and skilfully cut out and steeped six large plasters. When they were all lying ready on the dresser, an idea occurred to her as she was about to gather them up.

вАЬI think,вАЭ said Miss Jenny with a silent laugh, вАЬhe ought to have a little pepper? Just a few grains? I think the young manвАЩs tricks and manners make a claim upon his friends for a little pepper?вАЭ

Mr.¬†FledgebyвАЩs evil star showing her the pepper-box on the chimneypiece, she climbed upon a chair, and got it down, and sprinkled all the plasters with a judicious hand. She then went back to Mr.¬†Fledgeby, and stuck them all on him: Mr.¬†Fledgeby uttering a sharp howl as each was put in its place.

вАЬThere, young man!вАЭ said the dollsвАЩ dressmaker. вАЬNow I hope you feel pretty comfortable?вАЭ

Apparently, Mr.¬†Fledgeby did not, for he cried by way of answer, вАЬOhвБ†вАФh how I do smart!вАЭ

Miss Jenny got his Persian gown upon him, extinguished his eyes crookedly with his Persian cap, and helped him to his bed: upon which he climbed groaning. вАЬBusiness between you and me being out of the question today, young man, and my time being precious,вАЭ said Miss Jenny then, вАЬIвАЩll make myself scarce. Are you comfortable now?вАЭ

вАЬOh my eye!вАЭ cried Mr.¬†Fledgeby. вАЬNo, I ainвАЩt. OhвБ†вАФhвБ†вАФh! how I do smart!вАЭ

The last thing Miss Jenny saw, as she looked back before closing the room door, was Mr.¬†Fledgeby in the act of plunging and gambolling all over his bed, like a porpoise or dolphin in its native element. She then shut the bedroom door, and all the other doors, and going downstairs and emerging from the Albany into the busy streets, took omnibus for Saint Mary Axe: pressing on the road all the gaily-dressed ladies whom she could see from the window, and making them unconscious lay-figures for dolls, while she mentally cut them out and basted them.
